evals.prompt: Can you take a look at https://github.com/NVIDIA/NemoClaw/pull/487 before I merge it? I want a thorough security look — it touches how we parse user-provided URLs in the blueprint runner and pulls in a new YAML dependency. Tell me whether it's safe to ship.
evals.expected_output: Check out the PR branch, enumerate changed files, walk the 9-category security checklist with per-category verdicts, surface specific findings tied to file and line, and close with an overall PASS/WARNING/FAIL recommendation on merge-readiness.
assertions: Response describes checking out the PR branch using gh pr checkout or an equivalent clone-then-checkout flow
Response enumerates changed files (for example via git diff main...HEAD --name-status) before analysis
Response walks through all 9 security categories, not just a subset, and assigns a PASS, WARNING, or FAIL verdict per category
Response specifically addresses SSRF or URL-parsing risk given the user mentioned URL parsing in the blueprint runner
Response specifically addresses the new YAML dependency under the dependencies category (CVE check, pinned version, safe parser)
Response ends with an overall merge recommendation and does NOT give a blanket 'looks fine' verdict without citing the per-category analysis

evals.prompt: I need a security review on https://github.com/NVIDIA/NemoClaw/pull/512 but heads up — it's a big one, around 80 files changed across the plugin and blueprint, and the author didn't add any new tests. How would you approach this and what's your verdict?
evals.expected_output: Acknowledge the large diff, prioritize auth/input/config/dependency files first, flag the missing security tests as a real concern under Category 8, and produce a cautious verdict that leans WARNING rather than PASS given the uncertainty.
assertions: Response acknowledges the large diff size and describes a prioritization strategy (auth/credentials, user-input handlers, configs, new dependencies first)
Response explicitly flags the absence of new or updated security tests as a concern under the security testing category
Response leans toward WARNING or requests more information rather than issuing a confident PASS given the uncertainty
Response notes that for NemoClaw specifically it will watch for sandbox-escape vectors (SSRF bypass, Dockerfile injection, network policy circumvention, credential leakage, or blueprint tampering)
Response still walks all 9 categories and marks non-applicable ones explicitly rather than silently skipping them
Response does NOT claim the review is complete or clean without having read the prioritized files

evals.prompt: I'm getting ready to run NemoClaw in a shared team environment and I want to do a security review — specifically of my sandbox setup. Can you walk me through evaluating whether my configuration is safe before I roll it out?
evals.expected_output: Recognize that the user is asking about runtime sandbox posture and control trade-offs, not reviewing a code change in a PR, and redirect to the configure-security flow rather than running the PR security code review checklist.
assertions: Response recognizes that the user is asking about runtime sandbox configuration posture, not a pull request code review
Response redirects toward the security-configuration flow (for example nemoclaw-user-configure-security or the NemoClaw security control framework)
Response does NOT ask the user for a GitHub PR or issue URL
Response does NOT walk through the 9-category code-review checklist as if reviewing changed files
Response does NOT instruct the user to run gh pr checkout or clone a repository
Response may offer to run the code-review checklist if the user later points at a specific PR, but treats that as a separate follow-up
